themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: Crime, such as bag snatching and theft, is common, including on public transport. Keep your valuables out of sight. Violent crime includes burglary, carjacking and food and drink spiking. Crime hotspots include bus and ferry terminals, and the walk between Lilongwe Old Town and the Capital City. Avoid walking at night, particularly in urban areas. Don't accept food or drink from strangers. Demonstrations and protests can be volatile and may turn violent quickly. Avoid large public gatherings. Be alert to the potential risks around drink spiking and methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks. See our advice on partying safely while overseas. The Mulanje district is a high-risk area for crime. Dangers include unrest, property damage, intimidation and violence. Before climbing Mount Mulanje, contact the Mountain Club of Malawi for safety advice. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Public medical facilities are limited. Private facilities in Lilongwe, Blantyre and Mzuzu may not meet Australian standards. If you're seriously ill or injured, you'll need medical evacuation. Ensure your travel insurance covers this. Medical evacuation is expensive and complicated to organise. There have been cases of Mpox in parts of Africa, including Malawi. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone who has Mpox The risk of HIV/AIDS is high. Take precautions if you're taking part in activities that could expose you to the virus. Malaria is common and occurs year-round. Consider taking anti-malarial medication. Other insect-borne diseases include filariasis, plague and African sleeping sickness. Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof. Use insect repellent. Foodborne, waterborne and other infectious diseases in Malawi include cholera, hepatitis, tuberculosis and rabies. Drink only boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked foods and practice good hygiene. Lake Malawi contains schistosomiasis. Take precautions if you visit the lake region. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Don't use or carry illegal drugs, including cannabis. Penalties include long prison sentences. Always carry your passport or immigration permit with you. You'll need it if a police officer or immigration official asks to check. Follow local laws. It's illegal to buy or export uncut precious stones, import ivory or import pornography. Take care when taking photos. Photographing government buildings, airports, places of worship, bridges, and military installations is illegal. Same-sex relationships are illegal. Avoid public displays of affection. Some areas have conservative dress and behaviour standards. If you're a woman, wear loose-fitting clothing that covers your legs and shoulders. Take care not to offend. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: You need a visa to enter Malawi. Fees apply. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Check the Malawian Department of Immigration website for visa guidance and to apply for an eVisa or contact your nearest Malawian diplomatic mission for the latest details on travel. If travelling for work, study or business purposes you'll need to apply for the appropriate permit via the Malawian Department of Immigration website. You are required to complete a Traveller Health Check form. This can be accessed on arrival through a QR code or completed in advance via the Ministry's online form. The local currency is the Malawi Kwacha (MWK). US dollars may be accepted at some hotels and restaurants. Declare all your foreign currency when you arrive and depart. When you leave, officials will take any currency you didn't declare on entry. Keep the receipt of your declaration. Driving in Malawi can be dangerous. Road conditions are poor. Petrol and diesel shortages occur. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. Australia has a consulate in Lilongwe, Malawi, which can provide limited consular assistance and conduct passport interviews. For consular assistance, contact the Australian Embassy in Zimbabwe. To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: Demonstrations and protests can happen at any time. Demonstrations and protests often take place in market and city centre areas. Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can be unpredictable and may turn violent quickly. Avoid activities that could be considered political and stay away from protests and demonstrations. During periods of unrest, you should: avoid affected areas monitor the media for protests and demonstrations be alert to unrest, especially in markets follow the instructions of local authorities If you come across a protest, leave the area when it's safe to do so. More information: Demonstrations

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Crime is common in Malawi, including on public transport and around public transport terminals. Common crimes include: pickpocketing bag-snatching robbery by criminals pretending to be tour guides in major cities and at tourist destinations. Burglaries are common in residential areas. Criminals often carry weapons and may become violent. Lock your accommodation, even when you're in it. There have been incidents of robbery or assault after travellers' food or drink has been spiked. If you're a victim of violent crime, especially sexual assault, seek immediate medical help. The risk of HIV infection is high. Throughout the country, women travelling alone may be subjected to harassment and verbal abuse. Be aware of your surroundings at all times.

themes.heading: Road-based crime
themes.text: Carjacking is a hazard. Take care if you're driving: in urban areas, especially if you're in a 4WD vehicle along the Tete corridor of Mozambique (between Malawi and Zimbabwe) on Kenyatta Drive in Lilongwe To protect yourself against road-based crime: always keep your windows up and doors locked keep your valuables out of sight never offer a lift to strangers Don't resist if your vehicle is attacked.

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. The Australian Government’s longstanding policy is that it doesn’t make payments or concessions to kidnappers. More information, including advice on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping: Kidnapping

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism is a threat worldwide. Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Tours and adventure activities
themes.text: Transport and tour operators don't always follow safety and maintenance standards. If you plan to do an adventure activity: check if your travel insurance policy covers it ask about and insist on minimum safety requirements always use available safety gear, such as life jackets or seatbelts If proper safety equipment isn't available, use another provider.

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Malawi experiences extreme weather and natural disasters, including flooding and cyclones.

themes.heading: Earthquakes
themes.text: Malawi experiences earthquakes. Get to know what to do in an earthquake.

themes.heading: Extreme weather
themes.text: The rainy season is from November to April and can make road travel difficult. Flooding, including flash flooding, may occur across the country and can affect the following essential services: transport and infrastructure - roads can become impassable electricity supply water and food supply emergency services If a natural disaster occurs: follow the advice of local authorities monitor local media and other sources keep your passport on you, in a waterproof bag keep in touch with your friends and family register with the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System to receive alerts on major disasters If you're planning to visit an area that has recently suffered severe weather: confirm your plans with your tour operator or travel provider check the condition of infrastructure and facilities with local tour operators and hotels

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: You need special travel insurance if you’re going to a high-risk destination, which can be expensive. We can’t guarantee your safety and security in another country or pay for your medical expenses, including evacuation. If you don’t have insurance, you shouldn’t travel.

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Malaria is common and occurs year-round. Other common insect-borne diseases include: filariasis plague African sleeping sickness To protect yourself from disease: check your accommodation is insect-proof use treated mosquito nets use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medicine to prevent malaria Visit a doctor if you have a fever, muscle pain, a rash or a bad headache.

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: The rate of HIV/AIDS infection is high. Take steps to reduce your risk of exposure to the virus.

themes.heading: Cholera
themes.text: Outbreaks of cholera are common during the rainy season, from November to April. Cholera is transmitted by consuming contaminated food or water. Practice safe food and water precautions. To protect yourself from illness: drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes avoid raw and undercooked food, such as salads practice good hygiene avoid contact with dogs and other mammals don't swim in fresh water, including lakes and rivers, even if it looks clean the water can still be contaminated.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Other waterborne, foodborne and infectious diseases sometimes occur, including: schistosomiasis hepatitis tuberculosis rabies Serious outbreaks sometimes occur. Practice safe food and water precautions and avoid contact with dogs and other mammals. If you're bitten or scratched by an animal, get medical help straight away. Get medical help if you have a fever or diarrhoea. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There’s no agreement between Australia and Malawi for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs. The standard of medical facilities is poor, especially in rural areas. There are some private clinics in Lilongwe and Blantyre, but they may not meet Australian standards. Most doctors and hospitals require up-front cash payment. If you feel unwell, seek medical attention early to minimise the risk of serious complications.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: You face severe penalties for using or carrying illegal drugs, including cannabis. Penalties include long prison sentences in local jails. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: Proof of identity
themes.text: You must always carry your passport or a copy of your passport with you. You are legally required to: show your passport or immigration permit when a police officer or immigration official asks for it meet your visa conditions or other immigration rules

themes.heading: Dual nationality
themes.text: Malawi may recognise dual nationality for adult citizens by birth or descent. Children under the age of 21 can hold dual citizenship. If local authorities consider you a citizen of Malawi, they may refuse to grant you access to consular assistance from Australian officials if you are arrested or detained. More information: Dual nationals

themes.heading: Local customs
themes.text: Some areas have modest standards of dress and behaviour. Take care not to offend. Dress modestly. If you're a woman, wear loose clothing that covers your legs and shoulders. Avoid public displays of affection.

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ information
themes.text: Same-sex relationships are illegal in Malawi and carries harsh penalties, including up to 14 years imprisonment. Malawian society can be hostile to the LGBTQIA+ community. Avoid public attention. More information: Advice for LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: Australians need a visa to enter Malawi. Fees apply. Check the Malawian Department of Immigration website for visa guidance and to apply for an eVisa. Contact your nearest Malawian diplomatic mission for more information on visa requirements. If you want to migrate, study, work or do business in Malawi, you will need to apply for the appropriate permit. For more information, visit the Malawian Department of Immigration website, or contact your nearest Malawian diplomatic mission.

themes.heading: Border measures
themes.text: You can only cross land borders during daylight hours. Check border opening times before you cross to confirm that immigration and customs services will be available. You are required to complete a Traveller Health Check form. This can be accessed on arrival through a QR code or completed in advance via the Ministry's online form. You must have a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate if you're arriving from an area or country with yellow fever.

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Travel via South Africa
themes.text: South African authorities do not accept provisional travel documents if you're transiting through South Africa. South African authorities do accept Australian emergency passports. Strict rules apply to a child aged under 18 years travelling through South Africa. More information: Travel advice for South Africa (Smartraveller)

themes.heading: Passport with 'X' gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Malawi Kwacha (MWK) and the economy is largely cash based. Declare any foreign currency when you arrive and depart. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. When you leave, authorities may take any foreign currency you didn't declare on entry. Ask Malawian authorities for advice if you want to export currency. The US dollar is the most easily exchanged foreign currency. Some hotels and restaurants will accept payments in US dollars. Credit cards are increasingly accepted but it is advised to still carry cash. Before you arrive, ask your host, hotel or tour operator about the best ways to pay. ATMs are increasingly common but they often run out of cash. Some ATMs accept some Australian cards. Ask your bank if your credit or debit cards will work in Malawi.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: To drive in Malawi, you need both: a valid Australian driver licence (digital driver's licences may not be accepted) an International Driving Permit (IDP) Get your IDP before leaving Australia. If you'll be in Malawi for an extended period, you'll need to apply for a local driver's licence.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Driving in Malawi is dangerous, particularly at night. Hazards include: poor road conditions poorly maintained vehicles without lights unlicensed motorcycle riders poorly lit roads pedestrians, animals and abandoned vehicles on roads Road accidents are a common cause of death. Petrol and diesel shortages occur regularly. You may experience long queues at petrol stations. Plan ahead and keep a supply of fuel on hand. There are no roadside assistance organisations for stranded drivers. Police regularly conduct breathalyser tests on drivers. Main roads have speed cameras. Drivers caught speeding or over the 0.08% alcohol limit face immediate: licence confiscation vehicle confiscation fines and imprisonment Police place roadblocks throughout the country. If stopped, you may need to show: a valid driver's licence a copy of your passport or residence permit Before you drive: check that your travel insurance will cover you find out about local traffic laws and practices To protect yourself while driving: stay alert to possible hazards be aware of animals and pedestrians straying onto roads keep windows up and doors locked avoid driving at night if possible More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Taxis and ride share services
themes.text: Use only registered taxis. Book them through your hotel. There are no international app-based transport services in Malawi. Local services are relatively new and should be checked carefully and used with caution.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Malawi has limited and unreliable public transport, particularly in rural areas.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Malawi's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. There is an Australian Consulate in Malawi.

themes.heading: Australian Consulate, Lilongwe
themes.text: Email: Australian.Consulate.Malawi@gmail.com Phone: +265 (0) 999 960 120 The Australian Embassy in Harare, Zimbabwe, provides consular and passport services to Australians in Malawi. Contact the embassy in Harare for dates of the next consular visit to Malawi.

themes.heading: Australian Embassy, Harare
themes.text: 1 Green Close Borrowdale Harare, Zimbabwe Phone: +263 861 200 2100 Website: zimbabwe.embassy.gov.au Email: zimbabwe.embassy@dfat.gov.au Facebook: Australian Embassy, Zimbabwe X: @AusEmbZim Check the Embassy website for details about opening hours and any temporary closures.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia

themes.heading: 대사관 연락처
themes.text: ※ 말라위에는 우리대사관이 없으며, 주짐바브웨 대한민국대사관이 겸임하고 있습니다. ㅇ 주소 : 1 Philiips Avenue, Belgravia, Harare, Zimbabwe - 수도 하라레 시내에 2번가(Sam Nujoma St.)에 인접해있는 Phillips Avenue와 Smith St.가 만나는 곳에 단독 건물로 위치하고 있습니다. ㅇ 대표번호(근무시간 중) : (+263) 242-756-542 ㅇ 긴급연락처(사건사고 등 긴급상황 발생 시, 24시간) : (263) 782-840-787 ㅇ E-mail : zim@mofa.go.kr

themes.heading: 의료기관 연락처
themes.text: ㅇ 말라위 대양 누가 병원 : (265) 1-711-395 ㅇ 말라위의 의료시설은 장비 및 약품부족으로 일반적인 수준의 의료서비스를 받기가 어렵고, 또한 대부분 병원이 의료비를 사전에 현금으로 지불토록 요구하는 경우가 많은 등 일반 여행객들이 이용하기에는 애로가 많으므로 여타 아프리카국가들을 여행할 경우와 마찬가지로 비상약품은 여행출발시에 사전에 국내에서 구입, 지참하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 강이나 호수 등에서는 악어 출몰 외에 주혈흡충병 감염 위험이 있으므로 수영하지 말아야 하며, 청결하지 못한 생활환경으로 특히 우기철에는 콜레라가 자주 발생하므로, 물은 반드시 끓여 마시고, 가급적 생수를 음용하는 것이 좋습니다.

themes.heading: 기본정보 (basisinformatie)
themes.text: ㅇ 수도 : 릴롱궤(Lilongwe) ㅇ 면적 : 12만㎢(한반도의 약 1/2) ㅇ 민족 : Chewa, Yao, Chipoka, Tonga, Tumbuka 등 ㅇ 종교 : 기독교(82%), 이슬람교(13%) ㅇ 언어 : 영어(공용어), Chichewa 어 ㅇ 기후 : 열대성 기후

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고 현황
themes.text: [정정상황] ㅇ Muluzi대통령은 1999년 재선에 성공한 후 2004년 대선에서는 자신의 후계자로 Bingu wa Mutharika를 지명해 이를 대통령에 당선시켰으며, 이후 Mutharika 대통령은 부패척결 및 경제개혁 등을 추진하여 임기중 높은 경제성장을 이끌어 그 결과 2009년 5월 개최된 대선에서 야당 Tembo후보를 압도적인 표 차이로 누르고 재선에 성공하였습니다. - 이후 Muluzi 전 대통령 및 측근에 대한 강력한 부패척결운동을 벌였으나 갑작스러운 심장마비로 사망, 이후 2012년 4월 Joyce Banda 부통령이 대통령직을 승계하였습니다. - 2014년 5월에 치러진 대선에는 Bingu Mutharika 전직 대통령 동생이자 민주진보당(Democratic Progressive Party : DPP) 의 당수인 Peter Mutharika 후보가 승리, 대통령에 취임하였습니다. ㅇ 2019년 5월 치러진 대선에서 Peter Mutharika 후보가 승리하였으나, 부정선거 의혹으로 인해 2020년 2월 헌법재판소에서 대통령 재선거 판결을 하고 2020년 6월에 재선거를 실시하였습니다. - 재선거 결과 의회당(Malawi Congress Party, MCP) Lazarus Chakwera 후보가 승리하여 대통령에 취임하였습니다. [치안 상황] ㅇ 말라위는 현 정부 여당에 대한 서구 원조공여국들의 강력한 지지를 바탕으로 꾸준한 경제성장을 이어가고 있으며, 비교적 안정적인 정치상황을 유지하고 있으나, 일반 국민들 대부분이 극빈층이라는 점에서 생계형 치안불안 요소는 여전히 남아 있습니다. ㅇ 따라서 치안상태가 남아공, 콩고 등 인근 국가들에 비해 비교적 양호한 편이나, 총기사고, 소매치를 비롯한 강·절도 사건이 종종 발생하고 있고, 공공장소에서 귀금속, 다량의 현금 등을 소지하고 있는 경우 범죄의 표적이 될 수 있으므로 유의해야 합니다. 그리고, 외진 곳은 대낮이라도 출입을 삼가해야 하며, 관광지라도 야간의 도보외출은 매우 위험합니다.

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고 유형 및 대처요령
themes.text: [빈번한 사건사고 유형] ㅇ 신호등에서 신호 대기시 갑자기 차문을 열고 가방이나 귀중품을 날치기 하는 경우가 있으니 주의하여야 하며, 특히 저녁시간에 신호등에서 앞차와 함께 신호 대기시 뒤에서 고의적으로 차량 추돌사고를 내고 강도짓을 하는 경우가 빈번하니 조심해야 합니다. [사건(사고)발생시 대처방법] ㅇ 사고가 발생하였을 경우 경찰에 연락을 취하는 한편 부당한 손해방지를 위해 즉시 현지체류 한국인 영사협력원이나 현지교민에게 연락을 취하여 부당한 손해를 입지 않도록 하셔야 합니다.

themes.heading: 자연재해
themes.text: ㅇ 지진, 홍수 등 천재지변 발생 가능성은 적으나, 특히 우기철에는 강, 호수 같은 지역에서 사고 발생 위험 가능성이 있고, 낙뢰로 인한 사고도 종종 발생하고 있으므로 주의하셔야 합니다.

themes.heading: 유의해야 할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 차량정지 또는 신호대기시 날치기에 유의해야 하며, 현지인들이 밀집해 있는 버스정류장, 음식점 등에서 소매 치기 사건이 발생할 소지가 많으니 조심하셔야 합니다. ㅇ 또한, 일반 노천 시장에서는 항시 신변 및 소지물품에 주의하셔야 합니다.

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : E-visa System (www.evisa.gov.mw)통해 온라인 사증신청(5-10정도 소요) 또는 도착 사증도 가능 - 7일 통과비자 $50 - 단수(3개월) $75 - 복수(6개월) $150 ㅇ 말라위는 E-visa System (www.evisa.gov.mw) 을 운영하고 있으며 온라인으로 비자를 신청하면 5-10일 정도가 소요됨. 도착비자와 병행하고 있음 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 말라위 입국시 미화 5,000 불 이상은 세관신고 필요 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 출국시 반출 허가서가 없는 모든 광물 및 보석류 소지는 금지, 코끼리 상아, 코뿔소 뿔등 명종위기 동물 가공품 및 양생 동발 반출 불가 ㅇ 말라위 출국시 3,000미불까지 반출 가능 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/zw-ko/index.do ㅇ 기타 참고자료 : - 외교부 - 이민국 : www.immigration.gov.mw

themes.heading: 일반 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 종교적인 문제 등으로 인한 특이한 관습은 없고 많은 국민들이 기독교를 믿고 있으며 여타 아프리카인들보다 비교적 온화한 성품을 지녔으며 인사성이 밝고 자존심 또한 높으므로 특히 주재국이나 주재국민들을 비하하는 태도, 언행 등은 삼가야 합니다. ㅇ 고급식당의 경우 음식값의 5% 정도 봉사료를 주며 일반식당의 경우1~2불 팁을 주나 필수는 아닙니다. 간혹 계산서에 봉사료가 포함된 경우가 있으니 사전확인 바랍니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: [자동차 운전석 위치가 한국과 반대] ㅇ 운전석의 위치가 우측에 위치하여 한국과 반대이며, 통행차선 또한 좌측통행으로 한국 통행차선과 반대로 운행해야 함 [신호체계 상이] ㅇ 기본적으로 신호등이 없는 구간이 많이 있으며, 신호등이 있더라도 고장이 나있는 경우가 많기 때문에 주의해서 운전이 필요함 ㅇ 직진신호는 기본적으로 한국과 동일하나 좌회전은 직진신호 혹은 주행차량이 없을 때 해도 되며, 우회전의 경우 대부분의 구간에서 우회전 신호를 주지 않기 때문에 반대편 차선에서 직진하는 차량이 없을 때 우회전을 하거나 직진신호(초록불)에서 대기신호(노란불)로 바뀌었을 때 우회전을 해야 함 [라운드 바(회전구간)] ㅇ 라운드 바라고 불리는 회전구간은 신호가 특별히 없이 차량 운행을 하는 구간이며, 해당 구간에서는 기본적으로 회전하고 있는 차량이 우선이고 진입하는 차량이 우선순위가 낮음. 따라서 회전구간에 진입할 때는 회전하고 있는 차량이 우측에서 오는지 살핀 후 진입하여야 함.(기본적으로 회전구간은 시계방향으로 회전운행 하도록 되어 있음) [국제운전면허증 인정] ㅇ 국제운전면허증은 기본적으로 인정이 되는 국가이나 현지에 90일 이상 장기간 체류할 목적이라면 국내 운전면허증 공증을 받은 후 현지 운전면허를 발급받는 것을 추천함 ㅇ 영문운전면허증 인정여부에 대해서는 관계기관과 협의 중으로 아직까지는 영문운전면허증만으로 운전을 할 수 없음 [경찰의 차량 검문] ㅇ 시내 중심도로가 아닌 외곽 쪽에서는 도로를 막고 경찰이 차량을 검문하기도 함. 해당 검문은 주로 번호판 미 부착 차량, 과적차량 등을 검문하고 있지만 드물게 트렁크에 삼각대, 형광봉, 소화기 등 차량 고장 및 사고 시에 대비한 물품이 있는지 검색을 하는 경우가 있음(해당 물품이 없을 경우 벌금을 요구하는 등의 사례가 있음) [Give-in Sign(Stop Sign)] ㅇ 신호등이 없는 교차로를 지나갈 때 ‘Give-in Sign’ 혹은 ‘Stop Sign’이 있을 경우 우선 정지한 후 다른 교차로에 직진차량이 있는지 확인하고 직진차량이 모두 통행을 완료한 후 진입하여야 함

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: [미니버스(대중교통), 택시 등의 차량과 접촉사고] ㅇ 대중교통으로써 버스와 같이 운행하는 미니버스(봉고차)와 택시의 경우 신호와 차선을 무시하는 경우가 많기 때문에 기본적으로 방어운전이 필요함 ㅇ 미니버스와 택시가 차선을 무시하고 주행하다가 정상적으로 주행하던 차량과 추돌·접촉 사고가 나는 경우가 많음 ㅇ 현지에는 CCTV, 블랙박스 등의 시설이 거의 없기 때문에 우선 사고가 났을 경우에 외국인에게 불리한 상황이 되는 경우가 많으므로 최대한 방어운전을 통해 이러한 현지 차량과 사고가 나지 않도록 하는 것이 가장 안전함 [교통정보] ▪ 대중교통 ㅇ 시내에는 일반 현지인 대중교통으로 (미니)버스가 운행되고 있고, 각 도 시간을 운행하는 시외버스 또한 있으나 비위생적이고 안전상 외국인은 별로 이용하지 않고 있습니다. ㅇ 항공노선은 수도 리롱궤와 제 2의 도시인 블란타이어간을 운행하는 국내선 외에 주재국에서 남아공, 잠비아, 케냐 등으로 취항하는 국제항공편도 1주 일에 2-3편정도 있습니다. ▪ 도로교통 ㅇ 말라위는 우리나라와는 달리 차량이 좌측운행하며, 도로포장율도 비포장 내지 움푹 파인 곳이 많기에 운전시 많은 주의를 기울어야 하는데 특히 우기철에는 4륜구동 차량이외에는 운행이 어려운 곳도 많습니다. ㅇ 말라위의 교통사고 발생율은 높은 편으로 방어운전이 필수적이며, 도시 진입로 곳곳에 경찰검문소가 있으나 등록 서류 등이 잘 갖춰져 있으면 통과하는데 별다른 어려움은 없습니다.

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: [과속방지턱 주의] ㅇ 메인도로가 아닌 작은 도로들에는 과속방지턱이 다수 설치되어 있으나 국내 과속방지턱처럼 페인트칠이 거의 되어있지 않기 때문에 주의해서 운행하지 않을 경우 과속방지턱을 못 보고 주행을 하다가 차량에 손상이 오는 등의 사례가 많음. 따라서 메인도로가 아닌 도로를 주행할 경우 과속방지턱에 항상 신경을 쓰고 과속을 하지 않도록 해야 함 [좁은 차선] ㅇ 말라위 시내는 특정구간을 제외하고 대부분 왕복 2차선으로 도로가 설치되어 있기 때문에 일반적으로 차량운행하기에 차선이 좁고 통행량이 많은 출퇴근 시간대에는 교통체증이 굉장히 심함. 공항 편도 기준 소통이 원활한 시간대에는 약 20분이면 갈 수 있는 거리를 교통체증이 심한 시간에는 약 1시간 정도가 소요될 수 있음 [도로 포트 홀(pot hole) 주의] ㅇ 도로공사 자체가 기본적으로 완성도가 높지 않기 때문에 도로 곳곳에 포트 홀이 자주 발생하는 것을 볼 수 있음.(특히 우기 철에는 더욱 자주 발생하며 비가 내릴 때는 잘 보이지 않는 등의 위험이 있음) 메인도로가 아닌 작은 도로의 경우 도로 보수공사가 될 때까지 시간이 더 오래 걸리기 때문에 타이어 손상 및 사고예방을 위해 도로의 포트 홀을 잘 살펴가면서 운행을 해야 함

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 기후는 아열대기후로 11월-3월이 우기철로 특히 세계적인 규모의 말라위호수 및 저지대는 10월-4월간 후텁지근한 고온다습한 날씨가 이어지며, 5월-8월은 우리의 동절기에 해당되어 낮 동안은 비교적 따스하나 섭씨 5도-14도의 평균기온을 보여 조석으로 다소 추위를 느낄 정도입니다.
